title: Emergency Guide: Cat Violent Full Body Convulsions
meta_desc: Is your cat having violent convulsions? This is a high-level emergency. Learn what it means and why you must seek immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
What Are Violent Full-Body Convulsions?

Violent full-body convulsions in cats are typically generalized seizures, often referred to as grand mal seizures. These occur when a sudden surge of abnormal electrical activity in the brain causes involuntary muscle contractions, loss of consciousness, and sometimes loss of bladder or bowel control. We understand how distressing it is to witness your pet in this state, but it is important to stay calm so you can assist them effectively.

Is This a Medical Emergency?

Yes, this is a high-level medical emergency. While a single, short seizure is a serious concern, violent or prolonged convulsions can lead to hyperthermia (overheating), brain damage, or respiratory distress. If your cat is currently seizing, do not put your hands near their mouth, as they may bite transitionally. Clear away any sharp objects or furniture they might hit and transport them to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

If you are able to safely record a brief video of the event, it can be an invaluable tool for the veterinary team. Seizures often conclude by the time you arrive at the clinic, making it difficult for the vet to see exactly what happened. A video allows the veterinarian to analyze the specific movements and duration, helping them differentiate between a true seizure and other conditions like fainting or vestibular episodes, which ensures a faster and more accurate diagnosis.

faqs.question: Should I try to restrain my cat during a seizure?
faqs.answer: No. Do not attempt to hold or restrain your cat, as this can cause injury to both you and your pet. Simply ensure the environment is safe by moving hard or sharp objects away.

faqs.question: How long do these convulsions typically last?
faqs.answer: Most seizures last between 30 seconds and 2 minutes. If a seizure lasts longer than 5 minutes, it is a critical emergency known as status epilepticus.

faqs.question: What happens after the convulsions stop?
faqs.answer: Your cat will likely enter a 'post-ictal' phase where they appear disoriented, blind, or unsteady. Keep them in a quiet, dark room and proceed to the vet immediately.
